introSection: Wrath of Rakshasa is a steel floorless diving roller coaster currently operating at Six Flags Great America in Gurnee, Illinois, USA. The ride was built by Swiss firm Bolliger & Mabillard.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
The ride was announced on August 15, 2024.

In November, track for the coaster arrived on site and most of the work on footers had been completed.

As of December 5, 2024, the turn into the lift hill was completed. In addition, the supports for the brake run and the track connecting the brake run to the station was also finished.

As of December 8, 2024, part of the track for the brake run and transfer track had been placed.

On April 16, 2025, the ride had its first test with a train. It opened on May 31, 2025.

sections.title: Etymology
sections.content: Rakshasas are a mythological race of malevolent demons.

sections.title: Design
sections.content: Riders board from one side of the station and disembark via the other. Storage bins are provided. During the ride, the bins move from one side of the station to the other allowing riders to store and retrieve their belongings without having to climb over the train.

fullText:
Wrath of Rakshasa is a steel floorless diving roller coaster currently operating at Six Flags Great America in Gurnee, Illinois, USA. The ride was built by Swiss firm Bolliger & Mabillard.

The ride was announced on August 15, 2024.

In November, track for the coaster arrived on site and most of the work on footers had been completed.

As of December 5, 2024, the turn into the lift hill was completed. In addition, the supports for the brake run and the track connecting the brake run to the station was also finished.

As of December 8, 2024, part of the track for the brake run and transfer track had been placed.

On April 16, 2025, the ride had its first test with a train. It opened on May 31, 2025.

Rakshasas are a mythological race of malevolent demons.

Riders board from one side of the station and disembark via the other. Storage bins are provided. During the ride, the bins move from one side of the station to the other allowing riders to store and retrieve their belongings without having to climb over the train.
